summary: The post draws a parallel between Soviet centralized economic planning via computer systems (Gosplan) and modern Big Data infrastructure run by tech giants like Google and Facebook. While the Soviet approach was top-down rational modeling and Big Data uses bottom-up machine learning, both share the same goal: centralizing vast amounts of information for the purpose of societal control. The author argues this centralization has produced modern absurdities comparable to those of Soviet society.
key_points: Big Data and Soviet Gosplan share the same fundamental goal of centralizing information for control
The internet's distributed promise has been crushed by client-server Web architecture
Modern tech control is indirect and bottom-up through advertising, unlike Soviet top-down control
Centralization of information has produced societal absurdities analogous to Soviet-era distortions
The death of private life serves consumerism and economic growth
money_quotes: Big Data and the machine learning tools bring a ghostly vision to the software world that Soviet technocrats, who ran the Soviet Union, experimented with during the 60s and 70s.
The dream of the internet being a distributed system has been crushed by the client-server architecture of the Web.

Just as the centralized Soviet economy and computer systems produced gross absurdities in Soviet society, the centralization of information has produced vast obscurities in the lives of this generation.
The private life is dead, so that google and facebook can help others sell you things you don't need to keep the economy humming.
The control the Soviet Union used was straight-forward top down. The control modern western industry employs is indirect and bottom up.
key_insight: Modern Big Data centralization by tech giants is the Western capitalist mirror of Soviet central planning — different in method but identical in its ambition to control society through information, and equally capable of producing societal absurdities.
